title: Listening - Part 4: Learning a Traditional Craft
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people describing their experiences of learning a traditional skill or craft

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d spent my life in a high-tech job, but [21]I took up traditional stone-carving because I wanted to connect with the history of my local area[/21]. I wanted to feel like I was part of a long tradition. It’s incredibly physical work. [26]The most rewarding part has been the deep sense of satisfaction I get from creating something that will last for centuries[/26]. Unlike my digital work, which is obsolete in six months, these stones will be here long after I’m gone. It’s given me a whole new perspective on time and legacy. || Speaker 2: I’m a professional chef, and [22]I decided to learn the ancient art of knife-making because I wanted a deeper understanding of the tools I use every day[/22]. I wanted to be more self-reliant. It’s been a fascinating journey into metallurgy. However, [27]I found the extreme precision and patience required to be a real mental struggle[/27]. If you’re off by a millimeter, the whole blade is ruined. I’m used to the fast-paced, improvisational nature of a kitchen, so having to slow down and be so meticulous was a huge adjustment for my personality. || Speaker 3: I started learning traditional weaving [23]primarily as a way to manage the chronic stress I was experiencing in my corporate job[/23]. I needed something meditative. The repetitive motion of the loom is incredibly calming. [28]What I didn't expect was the massive boost in my ability to concentrate on complex tasks in other areas of my life[/28]. Because weaving requires you to keep track of intricate patterns for hours, my focus has improved significantly. I’m much more productive at work now, simply because I’ve trained my brain to stay present and attentive. || Speaker 4: I’ve always been interested in sustainable living, so [24]I took a course in traditional hedge-laying to help maintain the local environment in a natural way[/24]. I wanted to learn a skill that was being lost. It’s tough, outdoor work, especially in the winter. [29]The biggest hurdle for me has been the physical toll it takes on my joints and muscles[/29]. I’m not as young as I used to be, and I often come home feeling completely broken. But when I see the new growth in the spring and the birds nesting in the hedge I built, the pain seems like a small price to pay. || Speaker 5: I’m a graphic designer, and [25]I started learning letterpress printing because I was tired of the perfection of digital design and wanted to embrace 'happy accidents'[/25]. I wanted something more tactile. [30]The most significant outcome has been the wonderful new community of fellow crafters I’ve met at the studio[/30]. We share tips and tools and often collaborate on projects. It’s been a great way to escape the isolation of freelance work and feel part of a creative collective. It’s completely revitalized my passion for design in general.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what motivated each speaker to learn their craft.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 wanted to connect with the history of her local area and tradition.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted a deeper understanding of the tools he uses daily as a chef.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 learned weaving to manage chronic stress from her corporate job.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted to learn a skill to help maintain the environment in a natural way.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted to embrace 'happy accidents' and escape the perfection of digital design.

options.C: to start a profitable side business
options.E: to find a way to relax
options.F: to escape the limitations of modern technology

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as a significant result of their learning.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions the satisfaction of creating something that will last for centuries.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 found the extreme precision and patience required to be a mental struggle.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 mentions a boost in her ability to concentrate on complex tasks.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 mentions the physical toll the work takes on his joints and muscles.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 highlights the new community of fellow crafters she has met.

options.A: a change in their career direction
options.D: an increase in their physical flexibility
options.E: a surprising improvement in their focus
options.F: the physical strain of the activity
options.G: the challenge of the necessary attention to detail
options.H: the development of a new social network
